finding: On ID collision, each session-diff file is re-serialized with json.dumps then every id in id_map is applied via full-string str.replace, i.e. O(diffs x ids x len(diff)). For a large subtree with many colliding ids and large diffs this is quadratic-ish and re-scans the whole JSON string per id.
proposed_action: Replace whole-string replace-loop with a single structural walk that maps ids via id_map (recurse dict/list, swap exact-match string values/keys), or precompile one pass. Avoids re-serializing per id.

finding: load_export_file (read_text) and load_prior_context_files read entire files into memory; exports can be tens of MB and downstream builds full Turn lists. CPU+memory scale with transcript size.
disposition: Documented as a limitation in README (done in 1.0.3). A true streaming/incremental parse is a larger refactor; propose only if profiling shows real pain. Provide a measurement harness (PERF-6) first.

finding: db_move_project_metadata and db_move_session_metadata iterate sessions in Python and call Path(...).resolve() (filesystem syscalls) per row; db_move_session_metadata scans ALL sessions (SELECT id, directory FROM session) rather than only candidates under old_dir. O(N) stats per move on large DBs.
disposition: Avoid per-row resolve() (resolve the two prefixes once; compare/rewrite string prefixes without syscalls), and scope the session query with a WHERE directory LIKE prefix so only candidate rows are examined. Preserve current rebasing semantics.

finding: ocman_history.json 'runs' list grows unbounded; the whole file is loaded and rewritten on every deletion/cleanup/recovery. Cumulative totals are precomputed (good), but the runs array read/rewrite cost grows over time.
proposed_action: Cap retained detailed runs (e.g. keep last N, configurable) while preserving cumulative totals; or note the growth is bounded in practice. Low urgency for a single-user tool.

finding: Export writes one temp JSONL per table under tempfile.gettempdir() with a fixed name ocman_export_<table>_<session>.jsonl; concurrent exports of the same session (rare) could collide, and files persist if the process is killed mid-write. Not a speed issue but a resource-hygiene one adjacent to the perf hot path.
proposed_action: Use a per-run temp subdirectory (tempfile.mkdtemp) so names never collide and cleanup is a single rmtree. Low priority.

finding: No benchmarks/metrics exist to prove any optimization helps or to catch regressions. Perf claims are currently unverifiable.
proposed_action: Add a lightweight, opt-in benchmark (e.g. pytest that times import-collision remap and a large synthetic export parse) so PERF-1/PERF-2/PERF-3 changes are measurable. Keep out of the default fast suite.
